1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note this verse opening a new body of law — the holiness of Israel's conduct, set after the Day of Atonement. The witnesses note that Gill places it after the law of the day of atonement and its rites. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch head chapters 17–20 "Israel is not to walk in the way of the heathen and of the Canaanites, but in the ordinances of Jehovah." The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a new word — the LORD speaking again to Moses; the the place — set after the Day of Atonement; the the theme — holiness of life, that Israel walk not as the heathen but in the ordinances of Jehovah; the the scope — chapters 17–20 governing food, blood, and worship.

The grammatical commentators (Keil and Delitzsch) set the theme of all that follows: holiness of life — holiness of food, that the Israelites slaughter their animals only before the tabernacle as offerings, the blood and fat given to Jehovah, sacrificing no more to field-devils, and eating neither blood nor carrion.

So the witnesses make the verse the preface to the laws of holiness: The witnesses note the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The witnesses note the the place: set after the Day of Atonement. The witnesses note the the theme: holiness of life, that Israel walk not as the heathen but in the ordinances of Jehovah. The witnesses note the the scope: chapters 17–20 governing food, blood, and worship. The expositors observe the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The expositors observe the the place: set after the Day of Atonement. The expositors observe the the theme: holiness of life, that Israel walk not as the heathen but in the ordinances of Jehovah. The expositors observe the the scope: chapters 17–20 governing food, blood, and worship. The believer is taught to mark the a new word, the the place, the the theme, the the scope, and to rest the weight of the matter upon the word of God. The verse thus records that the atonement just made is followed at once by the call to holiness — the pardoned people now bound to walk in God's ordinances and not in the way of the nations.
2:
"Speak unto Aaron, and unto his sons, and unto all the children of Israel, and say unto them, This is the thing which the LORD hath commanded, saying," The witnesses note the charge given to priests and people alike — Aaron, his sons, and all Israel — for the law of slaughter touched every household. The witnesses note that Gill notes Aaron and his sons, now made priests, whose office was to offer the people's sacrifices. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch mark the breadth of the address, to the whole nation, as the law bound every man who killed an animal. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the to Aaron and his sons — the priests who offer the sacrifices; the to all Israel — the whole nation bound by the law; the the commandment — "this is the thing which the LORD hath commanded."; the the breadth — a law touching every household that kills a beast.

The witnesses agree the universal address fits a law that no Israelite could evade: the slaughter of a beast for food was made a sacred act, to be done at the one altar, so that the whole people are summoned to hear and obey.

So the witnesses make the verse the universal charge: The witnesses note the to Aaron and his sons: the priests who offer the sacrifices. The witnesses note the to all Israel: the whole nation bound by the law. The witnesses note the the commandment: "this is the thing which the LORD hath commanded." The witnesses note the the breadth: a law touching every household that kills a beast. The expositors observe the to Aaron and his sons: the priests who offer the sacrifices. The expositors observe the to all Israel: the whole nation bound by the law. The expositors observe the the commandment: "this is the thing which the LORD hath commanded." The expositors observe the the breadth: a law touching every household that kills a beast. The believer is taught to mark the to Aaron and his sons, the to all Israel, the the commandment, the the breadth, and to rest the weight of the matter upon the word of God. The verse thus records that the law of slaughter was laid on priests and people alike — every Israelite summoned to hear, for the killing of a beast was made a sacred act.
3:
"What man soever there be of the house of Israel, that killeth an ox, or lamb, or goat, in the camp, or that killeth it out of the camp," The witnesses note the law reaching every man, high or low, who killed one of the three sacrificial beasts, whether within the camp or without. The witnesses note that Gill stresses it binds every man, "whether high or low, rich or poor." The witnesses note that Ellicott reads it of the three sacrificial quadrupeds intended for private use — "killeth," not "sacrificeth" — that may not be slaughtered within or outside the camp. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the every man — high or low, rich or poor; the the beasts — an ox, lamb, or goat, the sacrificial kinds; the the place — in the camp or out of the camp, that is, anywhere; the the sense — these clean beasts brought as offerings, not killed apart from the altar.

The Wesleyan reader (Wesley) clarifies the scope: not the killing of any beast for common use, which might be done anywhere, but the killing of these clean beasts for sacrifice — these must be brought to the door, that no private or idolatrous offering be made apart from the altar.

So the witnesses make the verse the law of slaughter: The witnesses note the every man: high or low, rich or poor. The witnesses note the the beasts: an ox, lamb, or goat, the sacrificial kinds. The witnesses note the the place: in the camp or out of the camp, that is, anywhere. The witnesses note the the sense: these clean beasts brought as offerings, not killed apart from the altar. The expositors observe the every man: high or low, rich or poor. The expositors observe the the beasts: an ox, lamb, or goat, the sacrificial kinds. The expositors observe the the place: in the camp or out of the camp, that is, anywhere. The expositors observe the the sense: these clean beasts brought as offerings, not killed apart from the altar. The believer is taught to mark the every man, the the beasts, the the place, the the sense, and to rest the weight of the matter upon the word of God. The verse thus records that no Israelite might slaughter a sacrificial beast where he pleased — the killing of the clean kinds bound to the one altar of God.
4:
"And bringeth it not unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, to offer an offering unto the LORD... blood shall be imputed unto that man; he hath shed blood; and that man shall be cut off from among his people:" The witnesses note the heavy penalty: to slaughter without bringing the beast to the door was reckoned as bloodshed, and the man cut off from his people. The witnesses note that Gill notes the LORD would have every sacrifice brought to the door, where the altar and priests stood ready. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch explain "blood shall be imputed," that is, the killing was reckoned as the shedding of blood, bloodguiltiness, punished with extermination (Gen. 17:14). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the requirement — every such beast brought to the door of the tabernacle; the the imputation — the killing reckoned as the shedding of blood, bloodguiltiness; the the penalty — that man cut off from among his people; the the ground — that the life of the beast was God's, not to be taken apart from his altar.

The witnesses agree the severity guards the holiness of life and of worship: to take the life of a sacrificial beast apart from the altar was to treat as common what God claimed as sacred, and such contempt was reckoned as the shedding of blood.

So the witnesses make the verse the imputed blood: The witnesses note the the requirement: every such beast brought to the door of the tabernacle. The witnesses note the the imputation: the killing reckoned as the shedding of blood, bloodguiltiness. The witnesses note the the penalty: that man cut off from among his people. The witnesses note the the ground: that the life of the beast was God's, not to be taken apart from his altar. The expositors observe the the requirement: every such beast brought to the door of the tabernacle. The expositors observe the the imputation: the killing reckoned as the shedding of blood, bloodguiltiness. The expositors observe the the penalty: that man cut off from among his people. The expositors observe the the ground: that the life of the beast was God's, not to be taken apart from his altar. The believer is taught to mark the the requirement, the the imputation, the the penalty, the the ground, and to rest the weight of the matter upon the word of God. The verse thus records that to slaughter a sacrificial beast apart from the altar was counted as bloodshed and punished by excision — so jealously did God guard the sacredness of life and worship.
5:
"To the end that the children of Israel may bring their sacrifices, which they offer in the open field, even that they may bring them unto the LORD... and offer them for peace offerings unto the LORD." The witnesses note the purpose of the law: to draw Israel's sacrifices in from the open field to the one altar, offered as peace offerings to the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes that before the tabernacle was reared they had offered in the field and on high places, but now this was made unlawful. The witnesses note that Ellicott gives the reason — to prevent the Israelites sacrificing to demons in the open field, their offerings now brought within the precincts of the sanctuary. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the brought in — sacrifices drawn from the open field to the LORD; the the one place — offered at the door of the tabernacle; the as peace offerings — presented unto the LORD at his altar; the the reason — to prevent sacrificing to demons in the open field.

The witnesses agree the law was a hedge against idolatry: by requiring every offering at the one altar, God cut off the secret, scattered worship of the field where the people had been prone to sacrifice to false gods, and bound all true worship to the place he had chosen.

So the witnesses make the verse the gathered worship: The witnesses note the brought in: sacrifices drawn from the open field to the LORD. The witnesses note the the one place: offered at the door of the tabernacle. The witnesses note the as peace offerings: presented unto the LORD at his altar. The witnesses note the the reason: to prevent sacrificing to demons in the open field. The expositors observe the brought in: sacrifices drawn from the open field to the LORD. The expositors observe the the one place: offered at the door of the tabernacle. The expositors observe the as peace offerings: presented unto the LORD at his altar. The expositors observe the the reason: to prevent sacrificing to demons in the open field. The believer is taught to mark the brought in, the the one place, the as peace offerings, the the reason, and to rest the weight of the matter upon the word of God. The verse thus records that all worship was bound to the one altar — a hedge against the scattered, idolatrous offerings of the field, true worship gathered to the place God chose.
6:
"And the priest shall sprinkle the blood upon the altar of the LORD at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, and burn the fat for a sweet savour unto the LORD." The witnesses note the priest sprinkling the blood on the altar and burning the fat for a sweet savour — God's own portions in every offering. The witnesses note that Gill takes the altar as the altar of burnt offering at the door of the tabernacle. The witnesses note that Ellicott describes the priest catching the blood in a bowl and throwing it upon the walls of the altar, and burning the fat. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the blood — sprinkled by the priest upon the altar; the the fat — burned for a sweet savour unto the LORD; the the place — at the door of the tabernacle, the one altar; the the reason — god's propriety in the blood and fat, the people reconciled.

The Wesleyan reader (Wesley) gives the reason of the law: God's propriety in the blood and the fat, with which he was well pleased and the people reconciled; these two parts are named as the most eminent and peculiar, though other parts also were reserved for God.

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD's portions: The witnesses note the the blood: sprinkled by the priest upon the altar. The witnesses note the the fat: burned for a sweet savour unto the LORD. The witnesses note the the place: at the door of the tabernacle, the one altar. The witnesses note the the reason: God's propriety in the blood and fat, the people reconciled. The expositors observe the the blood: sprinkled by the priest upon the altar. The expositors observe the the fat: burned for a sweet savour unto the LORD. The expositors observe the the place: at the door of the tabernacle, the one altar. The expositors observe the the reason: God's propriety in the blood and fat, the people reconciled. The believer is taught to mark the the blood, the the fat, the the place, the the reason, and to rest the weight of the matter upon the word of God. The verse thus records that the blood and the fat are God's own — sprinkled and burned to him — for in the blood is atonement and in the fat the choicest, both reserved to the LORD.
7:
"And they shall no more offer their sacrifices unto devils, after whom they have gone a whoring. This shall be a statute for ever unto them throughout their generations." The witnesses note the deep purpose of the law: that Israel sacrifice no more to devils, after whom they had gone a whoring. The witnesses note that Gill reads "devils" broadly — idols in general, for whoever worships not God worships devils; the calves of Jeroboam and the golden calf are so called. The witnesses note that the worship of false gods is named "going a whoring," a breach of the covenant bond. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no more to devils — the end of sacrificing to false gods; the going a whoring — idolatry as covenant-breaking unfaithfulness; the the sense of "devils" — idols generally, or the field-devils of the wilderness; the a statute for ever — binding throughout their generations.

Here the witnesses divide over the precise sense of "devils" (Hebrew se'irim): some, with Gill, take it of idols generally, even gross devil-worship such as the nations practised; while the grammatical readers render it "field-devils" or he-goat demons of the wilderness — but all agree the law was to wean Israel from every false worship to the LORD alone.

So the witnesses make the verse the forbidden worship: The witnesses note the no more to devils: the end of sacrificing to false gods. The witnesses note the going a whoring: idolatry as covenant-breaking unfaithfulness. The witnesses note the the sense of "devils": idols generally, or the field-devils of the wilderness. The witnesses note the a statute for ever: binding throughout their generations. The expositors observe the no more to devils: the end of sacrificing to false gods. The expositors observe the going a whoring: idolatry as covenant-breaking unfaithfulness. The expositors observe the the sense of "devils": idols generally, or the field-devils of the wilderness. The expositors observe the a statute for ever: binding throughout their generations. The believer is taught to mark the no more to devils, the going a whoring, the the sense of "devils", the a statute for ever, and to rest the weight of the matter upon the word of God. The verse thus records that the whole law of the one altar aimed to wean Israel from devil-worship to the LORD alone — idolatry being a spiritual whoredom against their covenant God.
8:
"And thou shalt say unto them, Whatsoever man there be of the house of Israel, or of the strangers which sojourn among you, that offereth a burnt offering or sacrifice," The witnesses note the law extended to the stranger as well as the Israelite: whoever offered a burnt offering or sacrifice must bring it to the tabernacle. The witnesses note that Gill addresses the charge to Aaron and his sons and the children of Israel. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read here the command that whoever offered a sacrifice and did not bring it to the tabernacle was to be exterminated, a prohibition of sacrifice in any other place whatever. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Israelite and stranger — both bound by the law alike; the any burnt offering or sacrifice — all offerings included; the the one place — to be brought to the tabernacle, none other; the the safeguard — sacrifice confined to the sanctuary, against false worship.

The grammatical commentators (Keil and Delitzsch) connect this with the fuller law of Deuteronomy 12, given to suppress the disposition to sacrifice to other gods and in other places; the binding of all sacrifice to the one sanctuary was the great safeguard of pure worship.

So the witnesses make the verse the one place of sacrifice: The witnesses note the Israelite and stranger: both bound by the law alike. The witnesses note the any burnt offering or sacrifice: all offerings included. The witnesses note the the one place: to be brought to the tabernacle, none other. The witnesses note the the safeguard: sacrifice confined to the sanctuary, against false worship. The expositors observe the Israelite and stranger: both bound by the law alike. The expositors observe the any burnt offering or sacrifice: all offerings included. The expositors observe the the one place: to be brought to the tabernacle, none other. The expositors observe the the safeguard: sacrifice confined to the sanctuary, against false worship. The believer is taught to mark the Israelite and stranger, the any burnt offering or sacrifice, the the one place, the the safeguard, and to rest the weight of the matter upon the word of God. The verse thus records that all sacrifice was confined to the one sanctuary, stranger and Israelite alike bound — the great safeguard against the scattered worship of false gods.
9:
"And bringeth it not unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, to offer it unto the LORD; even that man shall be cut off from among his people." The witnesses note the penalty repeated: he who offered and brought it not to the door, to offer it publicly to the LORD, was to be cut off. The witnesses note that Gill notes the bringing to the door meant offering in a public manner by one of the LORD's priests, that the man take not on himself the priest's office nor offer to an idol. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch repeat the sentence of excision for sacrifice offered elsewhere. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the to the door — the offering brought to the tabernacle; the by the priest — offered publicly, not by the man himself; the the penalty — cut off from among his people if he failed; the the guard — worship kept from private hands and idolatry.

The witnesses agree the requirement of a public offering by a true priest was itself a guard: it kept worship out of private hands where it might be perverted to idolatry, and bound it to the appointed mediation of the priesthood — a shadow of the truth that there is one way of approach to God.

So the witnesses make the verse the public offering: The witnesses note the to the door: the offering brought to the tabernacle. The witnesses note the by the priest: offered publicly, not by the man himself. The witnesses note the the penalty: cut off from among his people if he failed. The witnesses note the the guard: worship kept from private hands and idolatry. The expositors observe the to the door: the offering brought to the tabernacle. The expositors observe the by the priest: offered publicly, not by the man himself. The expositors observe the the penalty: cut off from among his people if he failed. The expositors observe the the guard: worship kept from private hands and idolatry. The believer is taught to mark the to the door, the by the priest, the the penalty, the the guard, and to rest the weight of the matter upon the word of God. The verse thus records that worship was bound to the appointed priest and place — a guard against private and idolatrous offering, and a shadow of the one way of approach to God.
10:
"And whatsoever man there be of the house of Israel, or of the strangers that sojourn among you, that eateth any manner of blood; I will even set my face against that soul that eateth blood, and will cut him off from among his people." The witnesses note the grave prohibition of eating blood, God himself setting his face against the offender, Israelite or stranger. The witnesses note that Henry marks this the third giving of the law against eating blood (Lev. 3:17; Lev. 7:26), besides the precept to Noah (Gen. 9:4), binding strangers too. The witnesses note that Gill notes it reaches every Israelite of every age, sex, and condition. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the any manner of blood — none of it to be eaten; the Israelite and stranger — the law binding all alike; the God's own threat — "I will set my face against that soul."; the the apostolic echo — blood for a time forbidden the Gentile converts (Acts 15:29).

The witnesses agree the severity — God setting his own face against the eater — marks a law of more weight than first appears; Henry observes that this binding of the stranger was one reason the apostolic council for a time forbade blood to the Gentile converts (Acts 15:29).

So the witnesses make the verse the prohibition of blood: The witnesses note the any manner of blood: none of it to be eaten. The witnesses note the Israelite and stranger: the law binding all alike. The witnesses note the God's own threat: "I will set my face against that soul." The witnesses note the the apostolic echo: blood for a time forbidden the Gentile converts (Acts 15:29). The expositors observe the any manner of blood: none of it to be eaten. The expositors observe the Israelite and stranger: the law binding all alike. The expositors observe the God's own threat: "I will set my face against that soul." The expositors observe the the apostolic echo: blood for a time forbidden the Gentile converts (Acts 15:29). The believer is taught to mark the any manner of blood, the Israelite and stranger, the God's own threat, the the apostolic echo, and to rest the weight of the matter upon the word of God. The verse thus records that God set his own face against the eating of blood — a law of deep weight, reaching even the stranger, and echoed in the apostolic decree.
11:
"For the life of the flesh is in the blood: and I have given it to you upon the altar to make an atonement for your souls: for it is the blood that maketh an atonement for the soul." The witnesses fasten on this as the great reason of the whole law of blood — the doctrinal heart of the sacrificial system, that life is in the blood and atonement is by blood. The witnesses note that Gill explains the animal life or soul is in the blood, the primary seat of life, citing even Harvey, who found the circulation, that the blood is the fountain of life, the first to live and last to die. The witnesses note that the reason God forbids the eating of blood is that he has appointed it for a higher use — to make atonement upon the altar. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the life in the blood — the animal life seated in the blood; the given upon the altar — god appointing the blood to make atonement; the life for life — the blood, as the life, given to ransom the soul; the the fulfilment — the blood of Christ, his life poured out, the true atonement.

The witnesses agree this verse unfolds the whole sacrificial principle and reaches its fulfilment in the gospel: because the life is in the blood, God gave the blood upon the altar to atone, life given for life; so without shedding of blood is no remission (Heb. 9:22), and the blood of Christ, his life poured out, makes the true atonement of which all the altars were the shadow (1 Pet. 1:19).

So the witnesses make the verse the reason of atonement: The witnesses note the life in the blood: the animal life seated in the blood. The witnesses note the given upon the altar: God appointing the blood to make atonement. The witnesses note the life for life: the blood, as the life, given to ransom the soul. The witnesses note the the fulfilment: the blood of Christ, his life poured out, the true atonement. The expositors observe the life in the blood: the animal life seated in the blood. The expositors observe the given upon the altar: God appointing the blood to make atonement. The expositors observe the life for life: the blood, as the life, given to ransom the soul. The expositors observe the the fulfilment: the blood of Christ, his life poured out, the true atonement. The believer is taught to mark the life in the blood, the given upon the altar, the life for life, the the fulfilment, and to rest the weight of the matter upon the word of God. The verse thus records that atonement is made by blood because the life is in the blood — life given for life, the whole sacrificial principle fulfilled in the shed blood of Christ, without which is no remission.
12:
"Therefore I said unto the children of Israel, No soul of you shall eat blood, neither shall any stranger that sojourneth among you eat blood." The witnesses note the conclusion drawn from the reason just given: because the blood atones, none of Israel, nor any stranger, shall eat it. The witnesses note that Gill notes the law, great or small, repeated for the reason now given, which was its true ground all along. The witnesses note that Ellicott explains that because blood is the vehicle of life and ordained by God to atone for life, the people are forbidden to eat it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no soul shall eat blood — the charge laid on all Israel; the nor the stranger — the sojourner bound by the same law; the the ground — because the blood is the vehicle of life, ordained to atone; the the apostolic echo — the same prohibition enforced by the apostles.

The grammatical reader (Ellicott) adds that the stranger is bound too, having submitted to the law of the land, lest his eating lessen the horror with which Israel regarded it; and notes the apostolic enforcement of the same prohibition (Acts 15:20; Acts 21:25).

So the witnesses make the verse the prohibition restated: The witnesses note the no soul shall eat blood: the charge laid on all Israel. The witnesses note the nor the stranger: the sojourner bound by the same law. The witnesses note the the ground: because the blood is the vehicle of life, ordained to atone. The witnesses note the the apostolic echo: the same prohibition enforced by the apostles. The expositors observe the no soul shall eat blood: the charge laid on all Israel. The expositors observe the nor the stranger: the sojourner bound by the same law. The expositors observe the the ground: because the blood is the vehicle of life, ordained to atone. The expositors observe the the apostolic echo: the same prohibition enforced by the apostles. The believer is taught to mark the no soul shall eat blood, the nor the stranger, the the ground, the the apostolic echo, and to rest the weight of the matter upon the word of God. The verse thus records that because the blood atones it must not be eaten — the law binding Israelite and stranger, and renewed by the apostles in the early church.
13:
"And whatsoever man there be of the children of Israel, or of the strangers that sojourn among you, which hunteth and catcheth any beast or fowl that may be eaten; he shall even pour out the blood thereof, and cover it with dust." The witnesses note the law extended to hunted game: the blood of clean wild beasts and fowl must be poured out and covered with dust. The witnesses note that Gill notes the form of speech marking the persons bound — Israelites and proselytes of righteousness. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads the statute now extended beyond sacrificial and home-slaughtered animals to all clean creatures taken in hunting. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the hunter — israelite or stranger who takes clean beast or fowl; the pour out the blood — the life not eaten but let run out; the cover it with dust — the blood buried, owned as sacred; the the lesson — reverence for the life that belongs to God, even in the field.

The witnesses agree the covering of the blood with dust was a solemn acknowledgment that the life belonged to God even in the field: the hunter, far from the altar, still owned the blood as sacred, pouring it out and burying it rather than treating it as common — a constant teaching of reverence for life.

So the witnesses make the verse the hunted game: The witnesses note the the hunter: Israelite or stranger who takes clean beast or fowl. The witnesses note the pour out the blood: the life not eaten but let run out. The witnesses note the cover it with dust: the blood buried, owned as sacred. The witnesses note the the lesson: reverence for the life that belongs to God, even in the field. The expositors observe the the hunter: Israelite or stranger who takes clean beast or fowl. The expositors observe the pour out the blood: the life not eaten but let run out. The expositors observe the cover it with dust: the blood buried, owned as sacred. The expositors observe the the lesson: reverence for the life that belongs to God, even in the field. The believer is taught to mark the the hunter, the pour out the blood, the cover it with dust, the the lesson, and to rest the weight of the matter upon the word of God. The verse thus records that even the hunter's game must have its blood poured out and covered — the life owned as God's and treated as sacred, far from the altar as near it.
14:
"For it is the life of all flesh; the blood of it is for the life thereof... Ye shall eat the blood of no manner of flesh: for the life of all flesh is the blood thereof: whosoever eateth it shall be cut off." The witnesses note the reason pressed home and made universal: the blood is the life of all flesh, therefore the blood of no creature is to be eaten. The witnesses note that Gill simply marks it the life of every animal. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch explain "its blood makes out its soul," the blood being the soul of the living being in the flesh; therefore it was not to be eaten of any animal, and even with non-sacrificial creatures was to run out upon the ground and be covered. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the life of all flesh — the blood is the soul of every living creature; the no manner of flesh — the blood of none to be eaten; the poured out — even non-sacrificial blood let run upon the ground and covered; the the penalty — whosoever eateth it shall be cut off.

The witnesses agree the law rests on a fixed principle of creation, not a passing custom: the life of every flesh is in its blood, and the blood as the life belongs to God; this is why the prohibition is absolute, reaching every manner of flesh, and why the penalty of excision is attached.

So the witnesses make the verse the universal ground: The witnesses note the the life of all flesh: the blood is the soul of every living creature. The witnesses note the no manner of flesh: the blood of none to be eaten. The witnesses note the poured out: even non-sacrificial blood let run upon the ground and covered. The witnesses note the the penalty: whosoever eateth it shall be cut off. The expositors observe the the life of all flesh: the blood is the soul of every living creature. The expositors observe the no manner of flesh: the blood of none to be eaten. The expositors observe the poured out: even non-sacrificial blood let run upon the ground and covered. The expositors observe the the penalty: whosoever eateth it shall be cut off. The believer is taught to mark the the life of all flesh, the no manner of flesh, the poured out, the the penalty, and to rest the weight of the matter upon the word of God. The verse thus records that the blood is the life of all flesh and belongs to God — therefore the blood of no creature may be eaten, on pain of being cut off, a principle fixed in creation.
15:
"And every soul that eateth that which died of itself, or that which was torn with beasts, whether it be one of your own country, or a stranger, he shall both wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even: then shall he be clean." The witnesses note the law of carrion: he who ate of a beast that died of itself or was torn was defiled, and must wash and be unclean until evening. The witnesses note that Gill notes that even a little, the quantity of an olive, was a breach, such creatures having their blood not lawfully let out. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch join carrion and torn flesh as defiling, requiring washing and uncleanness until the evening. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the that which died of itself — or was torn with beasts, holding its blood; the Israelite or stranger — the law binding both; the washing — the eater to wash his clothes and bathe; the unclean until even — then clean again at evening.

The witnesses connect this law to the foregoing: the flesh of an animal that died of itself still held its blood, so eating it offended against the law of blood; Gill notes the tradition that Pythagoras took from this the rule binding his followers to abstain from all that died of itself.

So the witnesses make the verse the law of carrion: The witnesses note the that which died of itself: or was torn with beasts, holding its blood. The witnesses note the Israelite or stranger: the law binding both. The witnesses note the washing: the eater to wash his clothes and bathe. The witnesses note the unclean until even: then clean again at evening. The expositors observe the that which died of itself: or was torn with beasts, holding its blood. The expositors observe the Israelite or stranger: the law binding both. The expositors observe the washing: the eater to wash his clothes and bathe. The expositors observe the unclean until even: then clean again at evening. The believer is taught to mark the that which died of itself, the Israelite or stranger, the washing, the unclean until even, and to rest the weight of the matter upon the word of God. The verse thus records that to eat of carrion, whose blood was not let out, defiled the eater and required cleansing — the law of blood reaching even to the beast that died of itself.
16:
"But if he wash them not, nor bathe his flesh; then he shall bear his iniquity." The witnesses note the closing sanction: if the defiled man neglected the washing, he must bear his own iniquity. The witnesses note that Gill notes his guilt remained on him, and he would suffer the punishment due, whether by the hand of God or the magistrate. The witnesses note that Ellicott distinguishes the penalties: excision for entering the sanctuary defiled, stripes for partaking of the sacrificial meal unwashed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the if he wash not — the neglect of the appointed cleansing; the he shall bear his iniquity — the guilt remaining upon him; the the penalty — excision or stripes, by God or the magistrate; the the lesson — that provided cleansing must be used, or guilt remains.

The witnesses agree the law made the cleansing the responsibility of the defiled man himself: to neglect the appointed washing was to keep his uncleanness and bear its guilt — a solemn close teaching that provided cleansing must be used, and that contempt of it leaves a man in his sin.

So the witnesses make the verse the bearing of iniquity: The witnesses note the if he wash not: the neglect of the appointed cleansing. The witnesses note the he shall bear his iniquity: the guilt remaining upon him. The witnesses note the the penalty: excision or stripes, by God or the magistrate. The witnesses note the the lesson: that provided cleansing must be used, or guilt remains. The expositors observe the if he wash not: the neglect of the appointed cleansing. The expositors observe the he shall bear his iniquity: the guilt remaining upon him. The expositors observe the the penalty: excision or stripes, by God or the magistrate. The expositors observe the the lesson: that provided cleansing must be used, or guilt remains. The believer is taught to mark the if he wash not, the he shall bear his iniquity, the the penalty, the the lesson, and to rest the weight of the matter upon the word of God. The verse thus records that the man who neglected the appointed cleansing bore his own iniquity — a solemn close teaching that contempt of God's provided remedy leaves the sinner in his guilt.
